title: Feed-Forward Network
description: The token-wise transformation block inside transformer layers that refines each position after attention without mixing information across tokens.
openingSummary: A feed-forward network in a transformer block applies the same learned transformation independently to every token position after attention has mixed context across the sequence.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A feed-forward network is a stack of learned linear projections and nonlinear activation steps that maps one vector to another. Inside a transformer block it runs token-wise: each token vector is transformed on its own without reading neighboring positions. Papers and model cards refer to the same block as a feed-forward network; once that full name is clear, they often shorten it to FFN.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Attention lets token positions exchange context across the sequence, but the feed-forward network is the step that follows. It applies the same learned transformation independently to every position after that mixing. The block expands each token vector into a wider hidden size, passes it through a nonlinear activation function, and projects back to model width so the stack can compute richer per-token features. Without this step, attention would mostly rearrange information between positions rather than reshape what each position represents.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: After a self-attention layer lets every token read context from other tokens, the feed-forward network handles each position separately. Token five might represent the word "river": its vector expands to a wider hidden state, passes through an activation function such as GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit), and compresses back to model width before the next sublayer. Token six runs the same weight matrices on its own vector values, without mixing activations across positions.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: A transformer feed-forward network is not the same thing as the entire transformer. It is one sublayer inside each block, paired with attention and usually wrapped by residual connections and normalization. It is also not recurrent: during the forward pass it does not send activations back into earlier positions. Dense standard FFN, gated SwiGLU blocks, and sparse mixture-of-experts variants all play this same position-wise role with different internal wiring.
